meta.title: FaithWall vs Glorify: Two Different Jobs (and Why You Might Keep Both)
meta.description: FaithWall vs Glorify — one keeps Scripture on your lock screen all day, the other guides your sit-down devotional. Here's which to pick, and why both fit.

sections.title: Quick Answer
sections.content: **FaithWall vs Glorify** isn't really a contest — they do different jobs. Glorify is a sit-down Christian wellbeing app for daily devotionals, worship, and guided meditation. FaithWall puts a verse on your lock screen so Scripture reaches you all day without opening anything. Want depth at one set time? Glorify. Want exposure every time you glance at your phone? FaithWall. Most people keep both.

sections.text: If you're weighing **FaithWall vs Glorify**, you've probably noticed they get lumped together as 'Christian apps' — but they solve opposite halves of the same problem. Glorify is for the ten focused minutes you set aside. FaithWall is for the other twenty-three hours and fifty minutes. The average American picks up their phone **144 times a day**, per Reviews.org's 2026 Phone Habits report, and that gap is exactly where FaithWall lives.

Glorify is a polished **Christian wellbeing app** — daily devotionals, worship music, guided prayer, and wind-down meditations, wrapped in a calm, premium feel. It's genuinely good at what it does: it gives your quiet time a structure and a soundtrack. If you want a guided, sit-down devotional each morning, Glorify earns its place. The catch is the one every in-app experience shares — you have to remember to open it.

FaithWall does one thing on purpose. It puts daily Scripture on the **iPhone lock screen** — the surface you already see more than any other — and rotates it automatically through a themed pack. There's no session to start, no streak to protect, no audio to press play on. You pick up your phone to check the time, and a verse is already there. It's the daily-exposure piece an open-the-app devotional can't cover by design.
The real difference: opened vs. seen
The whole **FaithWall vs Glorify** question reduces to one distinction: *opened* versus *seen*. One waits for you to come to it; the other comes to you.

items: **Glorify** — depth at one moment. You sit, you open it, you engage for a set block of time.
**FaithWall** — exposure across the whole day. Passive, ambient, on the surface you can't avoid.
**Glorify** asks for your attention. **FaithWall** meets the attention you already give your phone.
**The overlap is small** — one is a destination, the other is a backdrop.

sections.text: Here's the failure mode nobody mentions when you download a devotional app: the morning gets away from you, you skip a day, the notification guilt builds, and by week three Glorify is another icon you swipe past. Not because it's bad — because **any app that requires opening competes with every other app on your phone**, and the feed usually wins. The devotional you did at 7am also tends to evaporate by 9. It lived inside an app you've since closed.

That's the gap FaithWall closes, and why it's less a rival to Glorify than the layer underneath it. You can't forget to open a lock screen. The verse is just there, 144 times a day, whether or not you had the discipline to start a session. **FaithWall** doesn't ask for the willpower that devotional apps quietly depend on.
But his delight is in the law of the LORD, and on his law he meditates day and night.

Start with **FaithWall**. It's free, installs in 60 seconds, needs no account, and it's the piece almost every phone is missing — Scripture on the surface you actually look at. Then, if you want a guided devotional with worship and meditation layered on top, add Glorify for your focused quiet-time block. FaithWall is the daily floor; Glorify is the depth you reach for when you have ten quiet minutes.

items: Install **FaithWall** first — pick a themed pack (Anxiety, Strength, Gratitude, Grief) and tap Set Lock Screen.
Let it rotate a verse onto your lock screen automatically; that's your all-day baseline.
Add Glorify if you want a guided morning devotional and worship for sit-down time.
Pair both with a **Quiet Time** Focus mode (iOS 16+) so the devotional block stays interruption-free.

sections.content: **Pro move:** match the season. If Glorify's plan this month is about anxiety, set FaithWall to its Anxiety pack. Now the verses on your lock screen reinforce the devotional you're already doing — same theme, two surfaces, all day long.

Glorify is a destination you visit. FaithWall is the backdrop you live in front of. That's why pitting them against each other misses the point — the people who get the most from either tend to run both, one for depth and one for exposure.

sections.text: Read our [FaithWall vs Hallow breakdown](/blog/faithwall-vs-hallow) for the same logic with a different app — guided prayer there, guided devotionals here, FaithWall as the exposure layer beneath both. If you're assembling a full setup, [the best Christian app for iPhone in 2026](/blog/best-christian-app-for-iphone-2026) lays out the three-app stack, and [the best prayer app for iPhone](/blog/best-prayer-app-for-iphone) covers the prayer slot. For the bigger picture, see the [Christian app comparisons pillar](/christian-app-comparisons).

sections.title: Get FaithWall — the layer Glorify can't cover
sections.description: Free, 60 seconds, no account. Daily Scripture on the lock screen you check 144 times a day — the exposure piece every devotional app is missing.
